module.title: Module 2: Regulations — 14 CFR Part 107
module.description: A thorough study of the federal rules that govern commercial small-drone flight: applicability and definitions, registration and Remote ID, the remote pilot certificate, the core operating limitations (400 ft AGL, 100 mph, 3 SM visibility, cloud clearance), day/night operations, VLOS, operations over people and moving vehicles, accident reporting, and waivers — the single most heavily tested regulatory area on the UAG exam.
learning_objectives: Explain the applicability of 14 CFR Part 107 and define key sUAS terms.
Describe Part 107 registration and marking requirements and the three Remote ID compliance options.
State remote pilot certificate eligibility, recency, and remote PIC responsibilities.
Recall the core operating limitations of 107.51 (altitude, speed, visibility, cloud clearance) and apply them to scenarios.
Apply the rules for daylight, civil twilight, and night operations, VLOS, visual observers, and right-of-way.
Classify operations over people (Categories 1–4) and over moving vehicles, and identify accident-reporting triggers under 107.9.
Explain Part 107 waivers (107.200/107.205) versus airspace authorizations, and distinguish recreational §44809 flight from Part 107.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: Applicability, Definitions, and Falsification
learning_objectives: Identify which operations fall under 14 CFR Part 107 and which are excluded.
Define core terms: small unmanned aircraft, sUAS, remote PIC, person manipulating the controls, visual observer, and control station.
Explain the consequences of falsifying or altering certificates and records.

content.html:
What Part 107 covers

14 CFR Part 107 is the body of federal regulation that governs the operation of small unmanned aircraft systems (sUAS) for non-recreational (i.e., commercial, professional, civic, or business) purposes in the United States. If you are flying a drone to take real-estate photos, inspect a roof, map a job site, or conduct a survey, you are operating under Part 107. A small unmanned aircraft is one that weighs less than 55 pounds (including everything on board, such as the battery and any payload) at takeoff. The unmanned aircraft system (sUAS) is the small unmanned aircraft plus the associated elements — the control station, data links, and other equipment — needed to operate it safely.

What Part 107 does NOT cover

Part 107 does not apply to everything that flies. The rule specifically excludes: aircraft at or above 55 lb; recreational/hobby flying conducted under the statutory exception in 49 U.S.C. § 44809 (covered in Lesson 7); operations under a separate FAA exemption or certificate of authorization; amateur rocketry, moored balloons, kites, and free balloons; public aircraft operations (certain government/agency flights operating under a COA); and air carrier operations. The exam may ask you to recognize that a hobbyist flying for fun is not automatically under Part 107 — but note the catch in Lesson 7: break a recreational condition and the flight is judged under Part 107 anyway.

rows: An unmanned aircraft weighing less than 55 lb (including payload) at takeoff.
The small unmanned aircraft plus its control station, links, and equipment for safe operation.
The person who has final authority and responsibility for the operation; must hold a remote pilot certificate.
The individual actually flying the aircraft; may be unrated if directly supervised by the Remote PIC who can immediately take control.
An optional crewmember who assists the Remote PIC by seeing and avoiding other air traffic and hazards.
The interface (e.g., the controller/ground station) used to operate the small unmanned aircraft.

content.text: Weight test: 'Small' means LESS THAN 55 lb at takeoff, counting the battery and any payload. A 55 lb (or heavier) aircraft is outside Part 107 entirely.

content.html:
Falsification, reproduction, and alteration

Honesty with the FAA is itself a rule. 14 CFR 107.5 prohibits the fraudulent or intentionally false making of any record, report, application, or statement required under Part 107; the reproduction for fraudulent purposes of any certificate or authorization; and the alteration of a certificate or authorization. Doing any of these is a basis for the FAA to deny an application or to suspend or revoke any certificate or waiver you hold — and it can carry civil or criminal penalties. In short: do not lie on your IACRA application, do not doctor a waiver, and do not forge a knowledge-test report.

content.text: Falsifying records, reproducing a certificate for fraud, or altering a certificate (107.5) is grounds for denial, suspension, or revocation — and possible criminal liability.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Part 107 governs non-recreational sUAS operations; 'small' = less than 55 lb including payload at takeoff.
Part 107 excludes recreational §44809 flights, public aircraft, model rockets, kites, moored/free balloons, and air carriers.
The sUAS is the aircraft PLUS control station, links, and supporting equipment.
The Remote PIC holds final authority; an unrated person may manipulate controls only under direct, take-over-able supervision.
Falsification, fraudulent reproduction, or alteration of certificates/records (107.5) can lead to denial, suspension, or revocation.

flashcards.definition: An unmanned aircraft weighing less than 55 lb (including everything on board) at takeoff.
flashcards.hint_1: Think of the upper weight limit.

flashcards.definition: The small unmanned aircraft together with its control station, data links, and other equipment needed to operate it safely.
Prohibits fraudulent/false records, fraudulent reproduction of a certificate, or alteration of a certificate; grounds for denial, suspension, or revocation.
The individual flying the aircraft; may be unrated only if directly supervised by a Remote PIC who can immediately take control.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Registration, Marking, and Remote ID
learning_objectives: State the registration and exterior-marking requirements for Part 107 aircraft.
Explain that Part 107 registration is per-aircraft, unlike recreational registration.
Describe the three Remote ID compliance options and their differences.

content.html:
Registration and marking

Before you fly commercially you must register the aircraft with the FAA through FAADroneZone. Under Part 107, registration is per aircraft: every individual drone gets its own unique registration number. (Contrast this with recreational flyers under §44809, who may register once and apply that single number to every drone they own.) Registration is required for any sUAS weighing more than 0.55 lb (250 g) up to 55 lb; and notably, all Part 107 aircraft must be registered regardless of weight, because they are not recreational. The registration costs about $5 and is valid for 3 years.

The assigned registration number must be marked on the exterior of the aircraft so it is legible and readable without tools, and you must carry proof of registration (digital or paper) during operations.

content.text: Part 107 = each drone registered individually. Recreational (§44809) = one number covers all your drones. The exam tests this difference directly.

content.html:
Remote ID — the 'digital license plate'

Remote ID (RID) broadcasts identification and location information about a drone in flight, allowing the public and authorities to receive it. Broadcast enforcement began September 16, 2023: if a drone is required to be registered, it must broadcast Remote ID — unless it is flown entirely within a FRIA. There are three ways to comply:

  • Standard Remote ID drone — RID capability is built in. It broadcasts the drone's ID, the drone's location and altitude, the control-station (pilot/take-off) location, velocity, a timestamp, and emergency status.
  • Remote ID broadcast module — a retrofit add-on attached to a non-RID drone. It broadcasts the drone ID, the take-off location (not the moving control-station location), and velocity. When using a module, the pilot must keep the drone within visual line of sight.
  • Fly within a FRIA — an FAA-Recognized Identification Area, a defined geographic area (sponsored by community-based organizations or educational institutions) where drones without RID equipment may be flown, provided the aircraft stays within VLOS of the operator.

rows: Drone ID, drone location/altitude, control-station (pilot) location, velocity, timestamp, emergency status
Retrofit; must keep drone within VLOS (no control-station tracking)
Drone must stay within VLOS inside the FRIA boundary

content.text: A broadcast module reports the TAKE-OFF location, while a standard RID drone reports the live CONTROL-STATION (pilot) location. That distinction is a favorite exam item.

content.alt: Diagram comparing the three Remote ID compliance options
content.caption: Figure: Three Remote ID compliance paths — standard RID drone (broadcasts pilot location), broadcast module (broadcasts take-off location), and flying within a FRIA.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Register via FAADroneZone; under Part 107 each aircraft gets its own number, regardless of weight.
Mark the registration number on the exterior, readable without tools; carry proof of registration.
Remote ID broadcast has been enforced since September 16, 2023.
Three RID options: standard RID drone, broadcast module, or flying within a FRIA.
Standard RID broadcasts the live control-station location; a broadcast module broadcasts the take-off location and requires VLOS.

flashcards.definition: Under Part 107, every individual drone must be registered with its own unique number (unlike recreational, where one number covers all).
Broadcast Remote ID has been required (for drones that must be registered) since September 16, 2023.
A retrofit add-on that broadcasts drone ID, take-off location, and velocity; the pilot must keep the drone within VLOS.
FAA-Recognized Identification Area — a defined area where drones without RID equipment may fly while staying within VLOS.
A drone with built-in RID that broadcasts drone ID/location/altitude, control-station location, velocity, timestamp, and emergency status.

images_needed.caption: Three Remote ID compliance paths side by side: standard RID drone broadcasting pilot/control-station location, a broadcast module broadcasting take-off location, and a FRIA boundary with a non-RID drone inside.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: The Remote Pilot Certificate & Remote PIC Responsibilities
learning_objectives: List the eligibility requirements for a remote pilot certificate.
Explain aeronautical-knowledge recency (recurrent training every 24 calendar months).
Describe the remote PIC's responsibilities and who may operate the aircraft.

content.html:
Eligibility (14 CFR 107.61–107.65)

To obtain a Remote Pilot Certificate with a small UAS rating you must be at least 16 years old; be able to read, speak, write, and understand English (waivable only for a medical reason such as a hearing or speech impairment); be in a physical and mental condition to safely operate a small UAS; and either pass the initial aeronautical knowledge test (UAG) or, if you already hold a Part 61 certificate and are current on a flight review, complete the FAA's online Part 107 training course instead. You must also be vetted by the TSA (a background check that runs automatically during the application through IACRA).

rows: Read, speak, write, understand English (medical waiver only)
Physical and mental condition to safely operate
Pass UAG test (or online course if a current Part 61 holder)

content.html:
The certificate does not expire — but recency does

The remote pilot certificate has no printed expiration date. However, you must keep your aeronautical knowledge recency current every 24 calendar months. Since the 2021 rule change, recency is renewed by completing a free online recurrent training course on FAASafety.gov — there is no in-person retest. That recurrent course now includes a night operations knowledge area. The FAA does not mail a new certificate after recurrent training; you must retain and be able to present the course-completion certificate as proof of currency. '24 calendar months' runs through the last day of the 24th month after completion.

content.text: Recency, not the certificate, is what expires: complete free online recurrent training every 24 calendar months. No retest, no new card — keep the completion certificate.

content.html:
Who may operate, and the Remote PIC's role

Each operation must have one Remote PIC who holds a remote pilot certificate and who has final authority and responsibility for the flight (107.19). A person without a certificate may physically manipulate the controls only if a certificated Remote PIC is directly supervising and is able to immediately take over control. The Remote PIC must ensure the operation poses no undue hazard to people, other aircraft, or property; must have the ability to direct the aircraft to ensure compliance; must check that the aircraft is in a condition for safe operation; and must cease the operation if it would pose a hazard. The Remote PIC must also be available to present the certificate and authorizations for inspection upon FAA request (107.7).

content.text: An unrated friend can fly the drone only while a certificated Remote PIC directly supervises and can instantly take the controls. Otherwise, a certificate is required to act as pilot.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Eligibility: 16+, English, sound physical/mental condition, pass UAG (or online course for Part 61 holders), TSA vetting.
The certificate never expires, but aeronautical knowledge recency must be renewed every 24 calendar months via free online recurrent training.
Keep the recurrent course-completion certificate as proof of currency — no new card is issued.
Every operation has one Remote PIC with final authority and responsibility (107.19).
An uncertificated person may manipulate the controls only under direct, immediate-takeover supervision by a Remote PIC.
On request, the Remote PIC must present the certificate, authorizations, and records for FAA inspection (107.7).

flashcards.term: Minimum age for remote pilot certificate
flashcards.definition: 16 years old.
flashcards.hint_1: Same as a learner's permit in many states.

flashcards.definition: Must be renewed every 24 calendar months via a free online recurrent training course on FAASafety.gov; no in-person retest.
Remote PIC responsibilities — final authority and responsibility for the operation; must ensure compliance and a safe operation.
An uncertificated person may manipulate the controls only if a Remote PIC is directly supervising and can immediately take control.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Core Operating Limitations (14 CFR 107.51)
learning_objectives: Recall the maximum altitude (400 ft AGL) and the 400-ft-above-structure exception.
Recall the maximum groundspeed, minimum flight visibility, and cloud-clearance requirements.
Apply these limits to operational scenarios.

content.html:
The four numbers of 107.51

Section 107.51 sets the core operating limitations every remote pilot must memorize. These are among the most frequently tested numbers on the entire exam, so commit them exactly:

  • Maximum altitude — 400 ft AGL. You may not fly higher than 400 feet above ground level unless the aircraft is flown within a 400-ft radius of a structure, in which case you may fly up to 400 ft above the structure's immediate uppermost limit (this lets you inspect tall towers and buildings).
  • Maximum groundspeed — 87 knots (100 mph).
  • Minimum flight visibility — 3 statute miles, measured from the control-station location.
  • Cloud clearance — at least 500 ft below clouds and at least 2,000 ft horizontally from clouds.

rows: 400 ft AGL — or up to 400 ft above a structure if within 400 ft of it
≥ 500 ft below clouds AND ≥ 2,000 ft horizontally from clouds

content.text: The structure exception is altitude only: within a 400-ft RADIUS of a structure you may go 400 ft ABOVE that structure's top — it does not raise the speed, visibility, or cloud-clearance limits.

content.html:
Why these limits exist

The 400-ft AGL cap keeps drones well below the airspace where most manned aircraft operate, reducing collision risk. The structure exception recognizes that inspecting a 1,000-ft broadcast tower would be impossible under a flat 400-ft ceiling — but only when you stay close (within a 400-ft radius) to that structure. The 3 SM visibility and 500-below / 2,000-horizontal cloud-clearance rules ensure you can see and avoid other traffic and that manned aircraft descending out of a cloud have room before reaching your drone. The 100 mph / 87 kt speed cap keeps the aircraft controllable and within reaction time for see-and-avoid.

sections.title: Scenario: inspecting a 600-ft tower
sections.content: You are hired to inspect a 600-ft communications tower. Flying straight up over open ground you would be limited to 400 ft AGL. But if you operate within a 400-ft radius of the tower, you may climb to 400 ft above the tower's top — i.e., up to about 1,000 ft AGL — to inspect the antenna array. Stray more than 400 ft horizontally from the tower and you must drop back to the 400-ft AGL ceiling.

sections.title: Scenario: a cloud layer at 1,200 ft
sections.content: A broken layer sits at 1,200 ft AGL. To respect the 500-ft-below cloud clearance, your maximum operating altitude becomes about 700 ft AGL for clearance purposes — but you are still capped at 400 ft AGL by 107.51 anyway, so 400 ft remains your ceiling. The cloud rule matters most when it forces you lower than 400 ft, or constrains how close you may fly horizontally to building cloud.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Maximum altitude is 400 ft AGL, or up to 400 ft above a structure when within a 400-ft radius of it.
Maximum groundspeed is 87 knots (100 mph).
Minimum flight visibility is 3 statute miles from the control station.
Cloud clearance is at least 500 ft below and 2,000 ft horizontal from clouds.
The structure exception raises only the altitude ceiling, not the other limits.

flashcards.definition: 400 ft AGL — or up to 400 ft above a structure's top when within a 400-ft radius of that structure.
87 knots (100 mph).
3 statute miles, measured from the control-station location.
At least 500 ft below clouds and at least 2,000 ft horizontally from clouds.

images_needed.caption: Side-view diagram showing the 400-ft AGL ceiling over open ground and the 400-ft-above-structure exception within a 400-ft radius of a tall tower.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Day/Night Operations, VLOS, Right-of-Way, and Hazardous Operations
learning_objectives: Apply the rules for daylight, civil twilight, and night operations, including anti-collision lighting.
Explain visual line of sight (VLOS), the use of visual observers, and the one-aircraft-per-pilot rule.
State the right-of-way, careless/reckless, dropping-objects, alcohol/drugs, and moving-vehicle rules.

content.html:
Daylight, civil twilight, and night (107.29)

Since the rule change effective April 21, 2021, Part 107 operations are allowed during the day, during civil twilight, and at night. For operations during civil twilight and at night, the aircraft must have lighted anti-collision lighting visible for at least 3 statute miles, with a flash rate sufficient to avoid a collision. (The Remote PIC may reduce the lighting intensity only if doing so is in the interest of safety — e.g., when it impairs the pilot's vision.) Night flight no longer requires a waiver, provided the pilot has completed the updated training (the initial UAG test or recurrent training, both of which now cover night operations). All previously issued night waivers were cancelled when the new rule took effect.

content.text: Civil twilight AND night both require anti-collision lighting visible for at least 3 statute miles. Daylight operations do not require it.

content.html:
Visual line of sight and visual observers (107.31, 107.33, 107.35)

VLOS (107.31): The Remote PIC, the person manipulating the controls, and any visual observer must be able to see the unmanned aircraft with vision unaided (corrective lenses are allowed) throughout the entire flight — well enough to know its location, attitude, altitude, and direction, to scan the airspace for traffic, and to ensure it endangers no one. First-person-view (FPV) goggles may supplement but cannot replace VLOS. Visual observer (107.33): using a VO is optional; if one is used, the Remote PIC and VO must be able to communicate and coordinate. One aircraft per pilot (107.35): a person may not act as Remote PIC, or manipulate the controls, of more than one unmanned aircraft at a time.

Right-of-way and hazardous operations (107.37, 107.23, 107.25)

Right of way (107.37): you must yield the right of way to all other aircraft — manned and unmanned — and must not operate so close to another aircraft as to create a collision hazard. The drone always gives way. Careless or reckless (107.23): you may not operate in a careless or reckless manner, and you may not allow an object to be dropped from the aircraft in a manner that creates a hazard to persons or property. Moving vehicles (107.25): you may not operate the controls from a moving vehicle or aircraft, except over a sparsely populated area (and never while transporting another person's property for compensation or hire).

rows: Day/civil-twilight/night allowed; anti-collision lighting visible 3 SM required for twilight and night
Maintain VLOS with unaided vision (corrective lenses OK) throughout the flight
Visual observer optional; if used, RPIC and VO must be able to communicate
One unmanned aircraft per pilot at a time
Yield right of way to all other aircraft; avoid collision hazards
No careless/reckless operation; no dropping objects that create a hazard
No operating from a moving vehicle except over a sparsely populated area

content.html:
Alcohol and drugs (107.27 / 107.57)

You may not act as Remote PIC, manipulate the controls, or serve as VO if you are: within 8 hours of consuming alcohol ('8 hours bottle-to-throttle'); under the influence of alcohol; have a blood-alcohol concentration of 0.04% or greater; or are using any drug that affects your faculties in a way contrary to safety. These thresholds mirror the rules for manned pilots.

content.text: Alcohol limits: 8 hours bottle-to-throttle, no flying under the influence, and BAC must be below 0.04%. The same applies to anyone serving as a visual observer.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Day, civil twilight, and night are all permitted; twilight and night require anti-collision lighting visible for 3 SM.
Night no longer requires a waiver if the pilot has completed the updated (night) training.
Maintain VLOS with unaided vision (corrective lenses OK) throughout the flight; FPV cannot replace it.
One unmanned aircraft per pilot; visual observers are optional but must be able to communicate with the RPIC.
Always yield right of way to all other aircraft; never operate carelessly/recklessly or drop hazardous objects.
No operating from a moving vehicle except over sparsely populated areas; alcohol = 8 hr, BAC < 0.04%, no impairing drugs.

flashcards.definition: Daylight, civil twilight, and night operations; anti-collision lighting visible for 3 statute miles required for twilight and night.
Visual line of sight — RPIC/person at controls/VO must see the aircraft with unaided vision (corrective lenses OK) throughout flight.
A person may not operate or act as Remote PIC of more than one unmanned aircraft at a time.
Yield right of way to all other aircraft (manned and unmanned); do not create a collision hazard.
No operating from a moving vehicle or aircraft, except over a sparsely populated area.
No operating within 8 hours of alcohol, while under the influence, with BAC ≥ 0.04%, or while using an impairing drug.

lessons.title: Lesson 6: Operations Over People & Moving Vehicles, Preflight, and Accident Reporting
learning_objectives: Classify operations over people into Categories 1–4 and state the key criteria for each.
Describe the rules for operations over moving vehicles and for carriage of property for compensation.
Identify the preflight requirements and the accident-reporting triggers and timeline under 107.9.

content.html:
Operations over people — Subpart D, Categories 1–4

Effective April 21, 2021, Part 107 allows certain operations over people without a waiver, sorted into four categories by the likely severity of injury the aircraft could cause. The category determines what you may fly over (a few transiting people, a closed site, or an open-air assembly):

  • Category 1: aircraft weighs 0.55 lb (250 g) or less at takeoff and throughout flight, has no exposed rotating parts that could lacerate skin, and meets Remote ID. The lightest, simplest category.
  • Category 2: the aircraft will not cause injury equal to or greater than the impact of 11 ft-lb of kinetic energy from a rigid object, has no lacerating exposed parts, and is built to an FAA-accepted means of compliance with a manufacturer declaration.
  • Category 3: the aircraft will not cause injury equal to or greater than 25 ft-lb of kinetic energy, with no lacerating parts and a declaration. No sustained flight over open-air assemblies is allowed — you may operate over people only within a closed/restricted-access site or while transiting.
  • Category 4: the aircraft holds an airworthiness certificate and is operated per its approved Flight Manual with ongoing maintenance. The most capable category; may operate over open-air assemblies if it also meets standard Remote ID.

rows: ≤ 0.55 lb, no exposed lacerating parts, meets RID
No injury ≥ 11 ft-lb KE; no lacerating parts; declaration
No injury ≥ 25 ft-lb KE; no lacerating parts; declaration
No — closed/restricted site or transit only

content.text: Remember the numbers: Cat 1 = 0.55 lb; Cat 2 = 11 ft-lb; Cat 3 = 25 ft-lb; Cat 4 = airworthiness certificate. Only Category 3 is barred from sustained flight over open-air assemblies.

content.html:
Operations over moving vehicles & carriage of property (107.145, 107.36)

For operations over moving vehicles (107.145): under Categories 1, 2, or 3 the aircraft must either remain within or over a closed- or restricted-access site (with the occupants on notice) or not maintain sustained flight over the moving vehicles; a Category 4 aircraft operates per its operating limitations. You may transport property for compensation or hire under Part 107 subject to weight (the total sUAS must stay under 55 lb), interstate limits, and the rule that 107.25 (moving vehicle) and 107.31 (VLOS) are not waivable when carrying someone else's property for hire. Carriage of hazardous material (107.36) is prohibited.

Preflight: condition for safe operation (107.15, 107.49)

Before each flight the Remote PIC must perform the actions of 107.49 — assess the operating environment (weather, airspace and any required authorizations, NOTAMs/TFRs, and the location of persons and property), confirm there is sufficient power for the intended operation, ensure any Remote ID equipment functions, and inspect the sUAS to confirm it is in a condition for safe operation (107.15). If the aircraft is not in a condition for safe operation, you must not fly it.

Accident / safety-event reporting — 14 CFR 107.9

This is a high-yield exam item. You must report an operation to the FAA no later than 10 calendar days after it results in at least one of the following: a serious injury to any person, or any loss of consciousness; OR damage to any property other than the small unmanned aircraft itself, unless the cost of repair (materials and labor) does not exceed $500, or the fair market value of the property does not exceed $500 in the event of a total loss. In short: report within 10 days for serious injury / loss of consciousness, or for property damage greater than $500 (excluding the drone itself).

content.text: 107.9: report within 10 calendar days if there is serious injury, loss of consciousness, OR property damage over $500 — and damage to the drone itself never counts toward the $500.

content.caption: Figure: 107.9 reporting decision flow — serious injury or loss of consciousness, or non-drone property damage over $500, triggers a report within 10 calendar days.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Operations over people fall into Categories 1–4 by injury potential: 0.55 lb / 11 ft-lb / 25 ft-lb / airworthiness certificate.
Category 3 may not sustain flight over open-air assemblies; Categories 1, 2, and 4 may (with RID).
Operations over moving vehicles require a closed/restricted site or no sustained flight (Cat 1–3); hazardous material carriage is prohibited.
Carrying property for hire is allowed but 107.25 and 107.31 are not waivable for it; the total sUAS must stay under 55 lb.
Preflight (107.15/107.49): inspect for condition for safe operation and assess weather, airspace, persons/property, power, and RID.
Report under 107.9 within 10 calendar days for serious injury, loss of consciousness, or non-drone property damage over $500.

flashcards.definition: Aircraft ≤ 0.55 lb (250 g) with no exposed lacerating rotating parts and meeting Remote ID.
flashcards.hint_1: Same weight as the registration threshold.

flashcards.definition: Cat 2 will not cause injury ≥ 11 ft-lb of kinetic energy; Cat 3 will not cause injury ≥ 25 ft-lb (and no sustained flight over open-air assemblies).
Requires an airworthiness certificate and operation per the approved Flight Manual.
Report to the FAA within 10 calendar days for serious injury, any loss of consciousness, or property damage (other than the drone) over $500.
Carriage of hazardous material by sUAS is prohibited.

images_needed.caption: Decision flowchart for 14 CFR 107.9: does the event involve serious injury, loss of consciousness, or non-drone property damage over $500? If yes, report within 10 calendar days.

lessons.title: Lesson 7: Waivers, Airspace Authorizations, and Recreational §44809 vs. Part 107
learning_objectives: Explain the certificate-of-waiver process (107.200) and which sections are waivable (107.205).
Distinguish an operational waiver from an airspace authorization (LAANC/DroneZone).
Compare recreational flying under §44809 with Part 107 operations.

content.html:
Certificate of waiver (107.200) and waivable sections (107.205)

A Certificate of Waiver (107.200) lets the FAA authorize a deviation from certain Part 107 rules if the applicant demonstrates the proposed operation can be conducted safely under the terms and conditions of the waiver. Not every rule is waivable. 107.205 lists the sections the FAA may waive, including: 107.25 (operation from a moving vehicle), 107.29 (daylight — historical), 107.31 (VLOS), 107.33 (visual observer), 107.35 (one aircraft per pilot), 107.37(a) (yielding right of way), 107.39 (operation over people), 107.41 (operation in certain airspace), and 107.51 (operating limitations such as altitude/speed/visibility). Importantly, 107.25 and 107.31 are NOT waivable when you are carrying another person's property for compensation or hire.

content.text: Even for a waiver, you must show the operation can be done SAFELY. And 107.25 (moving vehicle) and 107.31 (VLOS) cannot be waived for carrying someone else's property for hire.

content.html:
Waiver vs. airspace authorization

Do not confuse the two. An operational waiver (above) deviates from a Part 107 rule. An airspace authorization is what you need to fly in controlled airspace (Class B, C, D, and surface-area E) and is obtained through LAANC (Low Altitude Authorization and Notification Capability — often near-real-time) or through FAADroneZone for airports not LAANC-enabled. In uncontrolled Class G airspace below 400 ft AGL, no ATC authorization is required. You may need both a waiver and an authorization for some complex operations, but they are separate approvals.

rows: Deviation from a waivable Part 107 rule (e.g., night before 2021, VLOS, over people)
Apply to FAA showing the operation is safe

content.html:
Recreational §44809 vs. Part 107

Hobby flying is governed by the statutory Exception for Limited Recreational Operations, 49 U.S.C. § 44809, not by Part 107. Recreational flyers must fly strictly for recreation, follow a community-based organization's (CBO) safety guidelines, pass the free TRUST test (no expiration), register (one number can cover all their drones), and stay within VLOS. Part 107 grants greater privileges — higher altitude/airspace access, the ability to fly for business, and the ability to apply for waivers — in exchange for greater vetting (the remote pilot certificate). A crucial catch: if a recreational flyer violates any §44809 condition, the flight is judged under Part 107 instead, exposing the pilot to greater requirements and penalties.

rows: Remote Pilot Certificate (UAG test + TSA vetting)
One number can cover all your drones
Break a condition → flight judged under Part 107

content.text: Break a single §44809 recreational condition and the FAA treats the whole flight as a Part 107 operation — which it cannot satisfy without a remote pilot certificate. This is a classic exam trap.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A 107.200 certificate of waiver permits deviation from waivable rules if the operation can be done safely.
107.25 and 107.31 are not waivable for carrying another person's property for hire.
Operational waivers (rule deviation) differ from airspace authorizations (LAANC/DroneZone for controlled airspace).
Recreational flying uses §44809/TRUST with fewer privileges; Part 107 offers more privileges with more vetting.
Violating any §44809 condition causes the flight to be judged under Part 107.

flashcards.definition: Certificate of Waiver — FAA may authorize deviation from listed Part 107 rules if the operation can be conducted safely.
107.25 (moving vehicle) and 107.31 (VLOS) cannot be waived when carrying another person's property for compensation.
The Exception for Limited Recreational Operations — governs hobby flight (TRUST test, CBO guidelines, VLOS).
If a recreational flyer violates any §44809 condition, the operation is judged under Part 107.

quiz_questions.question_text: You plan to fly a 4-lb drone to photograph a client's commercial building for an advertising brochure. Under which rule does this operation fall?
options: 14 CFR Part 107
49 U.S.C. § 44809 (recreational)
No rule applies because the drone is under 55 lb
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 14 CFR Part 107
quiz_questions.explanation: Flying for a business/advertising purpose is non-recreational, so it falls under Part 107. §44809 is only for strictly recreational flight, and all sUAS operations are regulated regardless of weight.

quiz_questions.question_text: A remote pilot intentionally enters false information on an IACRA certificate application. Under 14 CFR 107.5, this can result in:
options: Denial of the application or suspension/revocation of any certificate held
A written warning only, with no effect on the certificate
Nothing, because 107.5 applies only to manned pilots
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Denial of the application or suspension/revocation of any certificate held
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.5 prohibits fraudulent/false records and is grounds for denial, suspension, or revocation (plus possible criminal penalties). It applies to Part 107 remote pilots.

quiz_questions.question_text: An uncertificated coworker wants to fly the drone during a Part 107 job. This is permissible only if:
options: A certificated Remote PIC directly supervises and can immediately take control
The coworker has passed the TRUST test
The coworker stays under 400 ft AGL
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A certificated Remote PIC directly supervises and can immediately take control
quiz_questions.explanation: A person without a certificate may manipulate the controls only under direct supervision by a Remote PIC who can immediately take over. TRUST is for recreational flyers, and altitude limits do not substitute for the supervision requirement.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Part 107, how are drones registered?
options: Each individual aircraft must be registered with its own unique number
One registration number can be applied to all the pilot's drones
Registration is not required for Part 107
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Each individual aircraft must be registered with its own unique number
quiz_questions.explanation: Part 107 requires per-aircraft registration. Single-number-covers-all is the recreational (§44809) approach, and Part 107 requires registration regardless of weight.

quiz_questions.question_text: Remote ID broadcast became an enforced requirement for drones that must be registered on what date?
options: September 16, 2023
April 21, 2021
September 16, 2025
quiz_questions.correct_answer: September 16, 2023
quiz_questions.explanation: Broadcast Remote ID enforcement began September 16, 2023. April 21, 2021 was the effective date for night and operations-over-people rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: What key difference exists between a standard Remote ID drone and a Remote ID broadcast module?
options: A standard RID drone broadcasts the live control-station location; a module broadcasts the take-off location
A module broadcasts the control-station location; a standard RID drone broadcasts only the take-off location
Both broadcast exactly the same information
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A standard RID drone broadcasts the live control-station location; a module broadcasts the take-off location
quiz_questions.explanation: A standard RID drone (built-in) broadcasts the live control-station/pilot location, while a retrofit module broadcasts the take-off location and requires the pilot to keep the drone within VLOS.

quiz_questions.question_text: You want to fly a drone that has no Remote ID equipment at all. Which option makes that legal?
options: Fly within a FRIA, keeping the drone within VLOS
Fly only at night with anti-collision lighting
Register the drone twice
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Fly within a FRIA, keeping the drone within VLOS
quiz_questions.explanation: A FAA-Recognized Identification Area (FRIA) is the option that allows flying a non-RID drone, as long as it stays within VLOS inside the FRIA. Lighting and double registration do not satisfy RID.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the minimum age to be eligible for a Remote Pilot Certificate?
options: 16 years old
18 years old
14 years old
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 16 years old
quiz_questions.explanation: You must be at least 16 to hold a remote pilot certificate. (Recreational flyers under §44809 have no minimum certificate age but must take TRUST.)

quiz_questions.question_text: To keep a remote pilot certificate valid for operations, a pilot must:
options: Complete free online recurrent training every 24 calendar months
Retake the in-person UAG knowledge test every 24 months
Renew the certificate annually with the FAA, which issues a new card
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Complete free online recurrent training every 24 calendar months
quiz_questions.explanation: The certificate itself never expires, but aeronautical knowledge recency must be renewed every 24 calendar months via free online recurrent training — no in-person retest and no new card is issued.

quiz_questions.question_text: Who is ultimately responsible for ensuring a Part 107 operation complies with the regulations?
options: The Remote PIC, who has final authority and responsibility
The visual observer
The drone manufacturer
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The Remote PIC, who has final authority and responsibility
quiz_questions.explanation: Under 107.19, the Remote PIC holds final authority and responsibility for the operation and its compliance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pilot completed recurrent training and asks when a new certificate will arrive. What is correct?
options: No new certificate is issued; the pilot retains the course-completion certificate as proof of currency
A new physical certificate is mailed within 30 days
The certificate must be reprinted at a PSI testing center
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No new certificate is issued; the pilot retains the course-completion certificate as proof of currency
quiz_questions.explanation: The FAA does not issue a new certificate after recurrent training; the pilot must keep and be able to present the recurrent course-completion certificate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the maximum allowable groundspeed for a small unmanned aircraft under Part 107?
options: 87 knots (100 mph)
100 knots (115 mph)
60 knots (70 mph)
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 87 knots (100 mph)
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.51 limits groundspeed to 87 knots, equal to 100 mph.

quiz_questions.question_text: You must inspect the antenna at the top of a 700-ft tower. Under the structure exception, the highest you may legally fly is approximately:
options: 1,100 ft AGL, as long as you stay within a 400-ft radius of the tower
400 ft AGL, with no exception for structures
700 ft AGL, the height of the tower
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 1,100 ft AGL, as long as you stay within a 400-ft radius of the tower
quiz_questions.explanation: Within a 400-ft radius of a structure you may fly up to 400 ft above the structure's top: 700 + 400 ≈ 1,100 ft AGL. Outside the 400-ft radius, the 400-ft AGL ceiling applies again.

quiz_questions.question_text: The minimum flight visibility for a Part 107 operation, measured from the control station, is:
options: 3 statute miles
1 statute mile
5 statute miles
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 3 statute miles
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.51 requires a minimum flight visibility of 3 statute miles from the control-station location.

quiz_questions.question_text: What cloud clearance must a remote pilot maintain under 107.51?
options: At least 500 ft below and 2,000 ft horizontally from clouds
At least 1,000 ft below and 1 mile horizontally from clouds
Clear of clouds at all times with no specific distance
quiz_questions.correct_answer: At least 500 ft below and 2,000 ft horizontally from clouds
quiz_questions.explanation: The required cloud clearance is 500 ft below and 2,000 ft horizontal. The 1,000-ft/1-mile values apply to certain manned-aircraft rules, not Part 107.

quiz_questions.question_text: You want to operate at night under Part 107. What is required?
options: Anti-collision lighting visible for at least 3 statute miles, plus completion of the updated (night) training
A special night waiver, since night flight is otherwise prohibited
Nothing different from daytime operations
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Anti-collision lighting visible for at least 3 statute miles, plus completion of the updated (night) training
quiz_questions.explanation: Since the 2021 rule, night flight is allowed without a waiver if the aircraft has anti-collision lighting visible for 3 SM and the pilot has completed the updated night training. Night is not prohibited, but it does require the lighting.

quiz_questions.question_text: A remote pilot wears prescription glasses while flying. Regarding VLOS (107.31), this is:
options: Acceptable — corrective lenses are allowed; binoculars or FPV alone are not
Prohibited — vision must be completely unaided
Acceptable only if a visual observer is also used
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Acceptable — corrective lenses are allowed; binoculars or FPV alone are not
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.31 requires unaided vision except for corrective lenses. Binoculars and FPV goggles may supplement but cannot replace VLOS, and a VO is optional.

quiz_questions.question_text: A manned helicopter is approaching your drone's area. Under 107.37, you must:
options: Yield the right of way and maneuver to avoid a collision hazard
Hold your position and let the helicopter avoid you
Climb above the helicopter to stay clear
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Yield the right of way and maneuver to avoid a collision hazard
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.37 requires the unmanned aircraft to yield right of way to all other aircraft and avoid creating a collision hazard. The drone always gives way.

quiz_questions.question_text: Operating the controls of a drone from a moving vehicle is permitted under Part 107:
options: Only over a sparsely populated area (and not while carrying another's property for hire)
Anywhere, as long as someone else is driving
Never, under any circumstances
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Only over a sparsely populated area (and not while carrying another's property for hire)
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.25 prohibits operation from a moving vehicle except over a sparsely populated area, and never while transporting property for compensation/hire.

quiz_questions.question_text: A remote pilot finished two beers and wants to fly. Under Part 107, the pilot must wait at least:
options: 8 hours, and must not fly with a BAC of 0.04% or greater or while under the influence
4 hours, with no BAC limit
12 hours, with a BAC limit of 0.08%
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 8 hours, and must not fly with a BAC of 0.04% or greater or while under the influence
quiz_questions.explanation: The rule is 8 hours bottle-to-throttle, no flying under the influence, and BAC must be below 0.04%. 0.08% is the driving limit, not the aviation limit.

quiz_questions.question_text: You want to fly directly over a small group of people. Your drone weighs 0.5 lb and has no exposed parts that could lacerate skin. This best fits:
options: Category 1 operations over people
Category 3 operations over people
Category 4 operations over people
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Category 1 operations over people
quiz_questions.explanation: Category 1 requires the aircraft to weigh 0.55 lb or less with no exposed lacerating parts and to meet Remote ID — matching a 0.5 lb drone. Cat 3 and Cat 4 apply to heavier or certificated aircraft.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which Operations Over People category is prohibited from sustained flight over open-air assemblies?
options: Category 3
Category 1
Category 4
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Category 3
quiz_questions.explanation: Category 3 may operate over people only within a closed/restricted-access site or while transiting, and may not sustain flight over open-air assemblies. Categories 1, 2, and 4 may (with RID).

quiz_questions.question_text: During a flight your drone strikes a parked car, causing $800 in damage; no one is hurt. Under 107.9 you must:
options: Report to the FAA within 10 calendar days
Not report, because no one was injured
Report only if the damage exceeds $1,000
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Report to the FAA within 10 calendar days
quiz_questions.explanation: Property damage (other than to the drone) over $500 triggers a 107.9 report within 10 calendar days, even with no injury. $800 exceeds $500.

quiz_questions.question_text: Your drone is damaged in a hard landing, costing $700 to repair, but nothing else is harmed and no one is injured. Under 107.9 you must:
options: Not report — damage to the small unmanned aircraft itself does not count
Report within 10 days because the cost exceeds $500
Report immediately within 24 hours
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Not report — damage to the small unmanned aircraft itself does not count
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.9 excludes damage to the small unmanned aircraft itself from the $500 threshold. With no injury, loss of consciousness, or other property damage, no report is required.

quiz_questions.question_text: As part of preflight (107.15/107.49), the Remote PIC must, among other things:
options: Assess weather/airspace/persons and confirm the aircraft is in condition for safe operation
Only confirm the battery is fully charged
File a flight plan with ATC for every flight
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Assess weather/airspace/persons and confirm the aircraft is in condition for safe operation
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.49 requires assessing the operating environment (weather, airspace/authorizations, persons/property, sufficient power, RID) and 107.15 requires the aircraft be in a condition for safe operation. No ATC flight plan is required for typical sUAS operations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a waivable section under 107.205?
options: 107.31 (visual line of sight)
107.9 (accident reporting)
107.5 (falsification)
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 107.31 (visual line of sight)
quiz_questions.explanation: 107.31 (VLOS) is on the 107.205 waivable list. Accident reporting (107.9) and falsification (107.5) are not waivable.

quiz_questions.question_text: To fly in Class D controlled airspace near a towered airport, a Part 107 pilot needs:
options: An airspace authorization (e.g., via LAANC or DroneZone)
Only a certificate of waiver under 107.200
Nothing, because Class D is uncontrolled
quiz_questions.correct_answer: An airspace authorization (e.g., via LAANC or DroneZone)
quiz_questions.explanation: Controlled airspace (B, C, D, surface E) requires an airspace authorization via LAANC or FAADroneZone — separate from an operational waiver. Class D is controlled, not uncontrolled.

quiz_questions.question_text: When carrying another person's property for compensation, which two sections may NOT be waived?
options: 107.25 (moving vehicle) and 107.31 (VLOS)
107.29 (night) and 107.35 (multiple aircraft)
107.39 (over people) and 107.51 (operating limitations)
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 107.25 (moving vehicle) and 107.31 (VLOS)
quiz_questions.explanation: For carriage of another's property for hire, 107.25 and 107.31 cannot be waived. The others remain potentially waivable under 107.205.

quiz_questions.question_text: A recreational flyer under §44809 begins using the drone to inspect a paying client's roof. The flight is now judged:
options: Under Part 107, because the recreational conditions were violated
Still under §44809, as long as TRUST was completed
Under no rule, since the drone is small
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Under Part 107, because the recreational conditions were violated
quiz_questions.explanation: Flying for compensation violates the 'strictly recreational' condition of §44809, so the operation is judged under Part 107 — which requires a remote pilot certificate the flyer may not hold.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly distinguishes a 107.200 waiver from an airspace authorization?
options: A waiver permits deviation from a Part 107 rule; an authorization permits flight in controlled airspace
They are the same thing under different names
A waiver is only for recreational flyers; an authorization is only for Part 107
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A waiver permits deviation from a Part 107 rule; an authorization permits flight in controlled airspace
quiz_questions.explanation: A certificate of waiver (107.200) authorizes deviation from a waivable rule, while an airspace authorization (LAANC/DroneZone) permits operations in controlled airspace. They are separate approvals, both for Part 107 operations.
